original_abstract: Do parties listen to their voters? This article addresses this important question by moving beyond position congruence to explore whether parties respond to voters’ issue priorities. It argues that political parties respond to voters in their election manifestos, but that their responsiveness varies across different party types: namely, that large parties are more responsive to voters’ policy priorities, while government parties listen less to voters’ issue demands. The study also posits that niche parties are not generally more responsive to voter demands, but that they are more responsive to the concerns of their supporters in their owned issue areas. To test these theoretical expectations, the study combines data from the Comparative Manifestos Project with data on voters’ policy priorities from the Comparative Study of Electoral Systems and various national election studies across eighteen European democracies in sixty-three elections from 1972–2011. Our findings have important implications for understanding political representation and democratic linkage.
humanized_abstract: The extent to which parties appear to be responsive to citizens in representative democracies is contested. Often studies focus exclusively on the concept of position congruence, missing the importance of understanding voter issue salience. Using data from election manifestos of political parties and the policy preferences of citizens derived from voters surveys (through the Comparative Study of Electoral Systems and various national election studies) in eighteen European countries, covering sixty-three elections between 1972 and 2011 (via the Comparative Manifestos Project, CMP), the paper explores whether the parties react to voters’ priorities in different respects. We find, on the basis of our analyses of the degree of alignment between party and voter concerns, that parties respond to citizens through their election manifestos but in different respects, according to party type: larger parties in particular are concerned with voters’ preference of policy issues while government parties are concerned less with voters’ prioritisation of policy issues and more with other types of issue priorities, and that smaller parties respond more strongly in their preferred issue area but react less to citizens in other respects. These conclusions are of considerable import for the debates in political science relating to political representation and democratic linkages.
